text: The video shows an external view of the surgical setup for a right lower lobectomy, featuring a subxiphoid port, an anterior camera port, and two anterior utility ports.

The surgeon uses a bipolar Maryland dissector for atesiolysis, inserting it through an eight-millimeter robotic port or directly through the chest wall. The instrument is maneuvered with ease using wrist movements, allowing precise angling. The bipolar energy device coagulates small vessels with minimal energy displacement, controlled by a foot pedal.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to address an incomplete fissure by first taking the inferior pulmonary vein. The surgeon efficiently switches between a suction device and a standard diathermy, demonstrating the ease of instrument exchange at the patient's bedside.

The surgeon performs dissection around the anterior hilum using a Maryland dissector, precisely following the tissue planes. Bipolar fenestrated forceps are used for blunt dissection, resembling the precision of a robotic procedure.

The surgeon uses bipolar fenestrated forceps to dissect around the inferior pulmonary vein, preparing the path for stapling. Various wristed instruments, including a bipolar Maryland dissector and hook diathermy, are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector to carefully dissect the pleura over the anterior hilum, identifying the superior pulmonary vein and pulmonary artery. Fenestrated forceps are then employed to encircle the superior pulmonary vein, utilizing their articulated movement to achieve a ninety-degree angle for precise dissection around large blood vessels. The blunt tip of the forceps aids in avoiding damage to the vessels. The surgeon proceeds to staple the vessels, demonstrating the efficiency of performing the procedure bedside.

The surgeon uses a subxiphoid port to fire staples during the lobectomy. Lymph nodes are identified behind the superior pulmonary vein. Fenestrated forceps are employed to grasp the lymph nodes at various angles without causing damage, while the bipolar Maryland dissector is used to gently dissect them out.

The surgeon dissects around the anterior segmental pulmonary artery branch using alternating Maryland dissector and fenestrated forceps. The instruments' angulation aids in maneuvering around the vessel, creating a clear path for subsequent stapling.
The surgeon uses the Cavidian Tristapler with the Signia gun, introduced through the subxiphoid port, to fire staples during the procedure.

The surgeon begins dissection at the junction of the pericardium and fat, following the phrenic nerve upwards. Using the bipolar Maryland dissector and fenestrated forceps, they retract and dissect along this plane, achieving excellent angles. The innominate vein, rhema vein, and superior vena cava (SVC) are clearly visible as the surgeon removes fat between the innominate and rhema veins with subtle wrist movements.

The surgeon performs a thymectomy using a left-sided approach due to the presence of a left superior vena cava (SVC). The tumor is visible above the surgical instruments, with the SVC and phrenic nerve lying over it. Wristed Maryland dissectors and fenestrated forceps are used to navigate the tissue planes effectively.
